content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

May 31, 1977

Len Steinbarth
Whalen, Saatjian & Steinbarth
530 "B" Street, Suite 1530
San Diego, CA    92101

Dear Mr. Steinbarth:

     Thank you for your letter of May 20, 1977, regarding
the reporting requirements of the Political Reform Act.

     In answer to your first question, an official is not
required to file a Statement when the official's interests
change. When the next Statement is required to be filed,
the interests must then be reported and the date of acquisi-
tion or disposal must be indicated if it occurred during the
reporting period.

     In regard to your second question, the Political Reform
Act requires that when investments and interests in real
property are required to be disclosed, the interests of the
spouse and the dependent children must also be reported
(Government Code Section 82029) Therefore, the official is
required to disclose the separate property of the spouse that is
located within the jurisdiction.

     Regarding the interest which is located outside the
jurisdiction but the main office of which is located in the
jurisdiction, the Political Reform Act provides that reportable
interests include those which do business within the juris-
diction. (Government Code Section 82014) The staff is currently
drafting an opinion on the issue of what constitutes doing
business within a jurisdiction but our interim advice is that
a minimum contacts standard should be applied. It would appear
that pursuant to this standard an interest located outside
the jurisdiction but with its main office located within the
jurisdiction would have to be reported.

Page Two
May 31, 1977


     Finally, a community property interest in income of a
spouse is required to be disclosed, (Government Code Section
82030) but income which is a spouse's separate property need
not.  Thus, if the income derived from the spouse's separate
interests is the separate property of the spouse, it need
not be reported.

     I hope this has answered your questions.  If you have
any further questions, please do not hesitate to contact
this office.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Dawn Wiser
                                        Technical Assistance and
                                        Analysis Division

WHALEN, SAATJIAN & STEINBARTH
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
530 "B" STREET, SUITE 1250
CALIFORNIA FIRST BANK BUILDING
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101
TELEPHONE (714) 235-6231

RICHARD B. WHALEN
RAYMOND G. SAATJIAN
LEN STEINBARTH
ROGER B. RUFFIN
OF COUNSEL

SAN FRANCISCO OFFICE
235 MONTGOMERY STREET SUITE 708
RUSS BUILDING
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94104
TELEPHONE (415) 397-0860

May 20, 1977

IN REPLY PLEASE REFER TO:
San Diego office


Dawn Wiser
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Ms. Wiser:

     After talking to you today it is my impression that it
is best that I get written confirmation of some of the points as
to the filing requirements of Proposition 9. If you recall, my
client, who is a councilperson for the City of San Diego, has
been recently married. I must know the specifics in what she
must report of her husband's business investments and holdings.
The following are a list of questions I would appreciate if you
could answer:

     1. Must she file a statement showing change of status
prior to the next required filing period?
     2. Must she report her husband's business interests
that are separate property, but located within the
jurisdiction for which she holds office?
     3. Must she report her husband's business interests
that are outside her jurisdiction if the main office of
that business is located within her jurisdiction?
(i.e. The business is wholly located and operated
outside the jurisdiction but minimal business decisions
are made at a main office in San Diego.)
     4. Must she disclose the income off of dividends which
are generated from her husband's separate property?

     I would also appreciate it if you could provide me with
any written literature which would assist me in interpreting this
section of Proposition 9.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        WHALEN, SAATJIAN & STEINBARTH
                                        
                                        [signature]
                                        Len Steinbarth

LS:tf

sections.question: 1. Must she file a statement showing change of status prior to the next required filing period; 2. Must she report her husband's business interests that are separate property, but located within the Jurisdiction for which she holds office? 3. Must she report her husband's business interests that are outside her Jurisdiction if the main office of that business is located within her jurisdiction? (i.e. The business is wholly located and operated outside the jurisdiction but minimal business decisions are made at a main office in San Diego.) 4. Must she disclose his income on or dividends which are generated from her husband's separate property?
sections.conclusion: An official is not required to file a statement when the official's interests change; interests must be reported at the next required filing. The spouse's and dependent children's interests must be reported, including separate property within the jurisdiction. Interests located outside the jurisdiction but with main office within the jurisdiction would have to be reported under a minimum contact standard. Community property interest in spouse's income must be disclosed, but spouse's separate property income need not be reported.

sections.question_synthetic: A city councilperson who recently married seeks clarification on what business interests, investments, and income of her spouse she must report under the Political Reform Act, including separate property within her jurisdiction, business interests outside her jurisdiction with main office inside, and spouse's separate property income.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that no interim statement is required for interest changes, spouse's separate property within the jurisdiction must be disclosed, interests with main offices in the jurisdiction must be reported even if located outside it, and only community property income requires disclosure, not separate property income.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR quality is moderate with some scanning artifacts but content is legible. Document contains both incoming request (dated May 20, 1977) and FPPC response (dated May 31, 1977). Response clearly structured with point-by-point answers. Government Code section references provided (82029, 82034, 82050).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Must she file a statement showing change of status prior to the next required filing period; 2. Must she report her husband's business interests that are separate property, but located within the Jurisdiction for which she holds office? 3. Must she report her husband's business interests that are outside her Jurisdiction if the main office of that business is located within her jurisdiction? (i.e. The business is wholly located and operated outside the jurisdiction but minimal business decisions are made at a main office in San Diego.) 4. Must she disclose his income on or dividends which are generated from her husband's separate property?

CONCLUSION: An official is not required to file a statement when the official's interests change; interests must be reported at the next required filing. The spouse's and dependent children's interests must be reported, including separate property within the jurisdiction. Interests located outside the jurisdiction but with main office within the jurisdiction would have to be reported under a minimum contact standard. Community property interest in spouse's income must be disclosed, but spouse's separate property income need not be reported.

In answer to your first question, an official is not required to file a Statement when the official's interests change. When the next Statement is required to be filed, the interests must then be reported and the date of acquisi- tion or disposal must be indicated if it occurred during the reporting period. In regard to your second question, the Political Reform Act requires that when investments and interests in real property are required to be disclosed, the interests of the spouse and the dependent children must also be reported (Government Code Section 82029) Therefore, the official is required to disclose the separate property of the spouse that is located within the jurisdiction. Regarding the interest which is located outside the jurisdiction but the main office of which is located in the jurisdiction, the Political Reform Act provides that reportable interests include those which do business within the juris- diction. (Government Code Section 82014) The staff is currently drafting an opinion on the issue of what constitutes doing business within a jurisdiction but our interim advice is that a minimum contacts standard should be applied. It would appear that pursuant to this standard an interest located outside the jurisdiction but with its main office located within the jurisdiction would have to be reported. embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to questions about disclosure requirements for a city councilperson's spouse's business interests and income under the Political Reform Act, addressing timing of disclosure, jurisdiction limitations, and property classifications.
